titleStmt.title: Oxygen Reduction Reaction on Single-Atom Catalysts From Density Functional Theory Calculations Combined with an Implicit Solvation Model

analytic.title: Oxygen Reduction Reaction on Single-Atom Catalysts From Density Functional Theory Calculations Combined with an Implicit Solvation Model

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: We present a density functional theory study of the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) on a single atom catalyst embedded in graphene, namely, TM-N 4 -C (TM = Fe and Co), using the effective screening medium method combined with the reference interaction site model (ESM-RISM). It was found that Fe-N 4 -C and Co-N 4 -C show comparable ORR activities from the constant electrode potential simulations, in contrast to the results obtained using the constant (neutral) charge simulation, in which the superior performance of Co-N 4 -C has been predicted. The constant potential method allows the variable charge and thus results in a potential dependence of the reaction-free energies different from that with the constant charge method in which the potential dependence is included as an ad hoc manner. We suggest the importance of the variable charge in the simulation of the electrochemical reaction, which is enabled by ESM-RISM.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: It has been well established that the single-atom catalyst, particularly the transition metal (TM = Fe and Co) embedded in N-doped graphene, has the potential to reduce O 2 to H 2 O with appreciable activity and stability. [1][2][3][4] In the past decade, great research efforts have been dedicated to the development of this electrocatalyst, especially in searching and tuning the active center moieties of the TM-N x -C y (where x ∈ [1, 2, 3, 4,..] and y ∈ [edge, 8, 10, 12,..]), as an effort to optimize their intrinsic activity for the proton exchange membrane fuel cell (PEMFC) application. [5][6][7][8][9] The state-of-the-art of this single-atom catalyst, mainly the 4-fold coordinate metal atom with pyridinic nitrogen atoms in graphene (Fe-N 4 -C and Co-N 4 -C), exhibits initial activity comparable to that of commercial Pt/C and offers an appealing pathway to solving the energy and environmental problem. 3,10,11 However, substantial improvements in the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) activity and stability at lower energy costs, i.e., lower overpotentials, are still required to escalate the economic feasibility of the PEMFC system for going to scale. [12][13][14][15] The electrochemical ORR occurs at the electrode-electrolyte interface, and thus, both the electrode surface and electrolyte environment could play a vital role in the catalytic performance of the catalyst. [16][17][18] Especially, the ORR activity on this catalyst has been shown to have a prominent sensitivity towards the changes in the environment (e.g., pH values, ionic effects, and so on). [19][20][21][22] It is believed that, under the ORR reducing potential, the catalytic reaction site will gain more charges and the electrical double-layer (EDL) is reconstructed due to the interface polarization, affecting the ORR intermediates at the electrochemical interface, and thus influencing the catalytic activity. 23 However, it is challenging to experimentally characterize the detail of ORR under the working condition. Therefore, atomistic simulations that take into account the electrochemical environment, i.e., electrode potential and EDL, are desirable to better understand the underlying reaction mechanism of ORR.

The computational hydrogen electrode (CHE) model 24 is arguably the most widely used atomistic approach to studying the mechanisms of electrochemical reactions and exploring efficient electrocatalysts. Within the conventional CHE model, the catalyst and reaction intermediates are treated quantum mechanically (mostly) in the vacuum condition, and the effects of the applied potential and electrolyte concentration (pH value) are included posterior as an algebraic correction to the free energy. The solvent effect can be included in the CHE model through an implicit solvent model or explicit solvent through (either classical or quantum mechanical) atomistic simulations, but the latter can be computationally extensive.

There have been several methodological developments to include the effects of the electrode potential [25][26][27][28] and the electrolyte solution 26,[29][30][31][32][33][34][35] in simulations of electrode-electrolyte solution interfaces within the grand-canonical ensemble for electrons and/or ions. In particular, the progress of the hybrid quantum-mechanical and molecular-mechanical method, in which electrode surface and adsorbate(s) are treated quantum mechanically, while the electrolyte solution is described using continuum model or solution theory, 28,[32][33][34][35] is remarkable.

In this study, we investigate the mechanism of ORR on TM-N 4 -C (TM = Fe, Co) catalysts by means of the density functional theory (DFT) calculations combined with the effective screening medium (ESM) method and the reference interaction site model (RISM), 33 a hybrid DFT and an implicit solvation model. By employing the fictitious charge particle method developed by Bonnet et al., 27 a constant-potential (constant-µ e ) simulation (i.e., variable number of electrons/surface charge) is made possible. Furthermore, RISM allows for a variable number of electrolyte ions in solution in response to the surface charge, thus keeping the charge neutrality of the whole system and the electrode potential of the electrode-electrolyte solution system can be defined. 36

div.head: Computational method
div.p: We used the projector augmented wave (PAW) method 37 as implemented in Quantum-ESPRESSO code. 38,39 PAW potentials were adopted from pslibrary, 40 which were generated using the Perdew-Burke-Ernzheof (PBE) 41 generalized gradient approximation to the exchange-correlation (XC) functional. Wave functions and augmentation charge density were expanded in terms of a plane-wave basis set with the kinetic energy cutoffs of 80 Ry and 800 Ry, respectively. The Fermi surface was treated using the Marzari-Vanderbilt cold smearing with a smearing width of 0.02 Ry. 42 The revised PBE exchange-correlation functional of Hammer et al. 43 with the dispersion correction of Grimme 44 (RPBE-D3), which was proven 45 to show comparative accuracy with the BEEF-vdW functional. 46 We confirmed that the use of PBE potentials does not change the results significantly in the RPBE(-D3) calculations. 45 The TM-N 4 moiety and reaction intermediates were described by DFT using a graphene (5 × 5) supercell, which was in contact with the HCl solution at 298.15 K described by RISM.

The kinetic energy cutoff for the solvent correlation functions was set to 160 Ry. We used the vacuum/slab/solvent boundary condition to model TM-N 4 -C's and reaction intermediates, while solvated H 2 O and H 3 O + were modeled with the solvent/ion/solvent one. 36,47 The electrolyte solution was described with simple point charge and five-point intermolecular potential models with modified Lennard-Jones (LJ) parameters for H 2 O 36 and those for H 3 O + and Cl -adopted from Chuev et al. 48 and Smith and Dang. 49 The LJ parameters for C and N atoms were generated using the data from Ref. 50 , and those for Fe and Co atoms were generated using the interaction energy curves for a H 2 O on Fe-N 4 -C and Co-N 4 -C surfaces in the most stable configurations. See Table S1 for the LJ parameters generated and used in this study. We chose to use the HCl solution to represent an acidic environment.

Note that the electrolyte plays an essential role in determining the electrode potential and pH, but the poisoning effect of the anion reported in Refs. 51,52 was not considered in the modeling with plain RISM employed in the present study. A 4×4 k-point grid was used to sample the surface Brillouin zone. Structural optimization was performed until the residual forces became less than 2.57 × 10 -2 eV/ Å (10 -3 Ry/a 0 ). See the Supporting Information for further details of the calculation.

div.head: Electric double layer
div.p: We begin by describing the EDL formed at the HCl/graphene solution interface from our ESM-RISM calculations. In Figure 1 (a), the distributions of H 3 O + and Cl -in the HCl solution on Fe-N 4 -C with different electrolyte concentrations are depicted. We can see that ESM-RISM is capable of determining the detailed statistical description of the EDL structure for a given electrolyte concentration. We also plot the electrostatic potential of the system in Figure 1 (b), which shows the spatial variation of potential in the EDL depending on the electrolyte concentration, applied potential, and its convergence far from the interface. To illustrate how the electrolyte ions distribute and respond to the applied potential, we depict the spatial distributions of Cl -and H + /H 3 O + ions in the 0.5 M HCl in contact with OH-adsorbed Fe-N 4 -C at different potentials (Figure 2). As anticipated, we can see that as the potential is increased from 0 to 1.23 V vs RHE, the concentration of the Cl -ion near the surface increases, while H + /H 3 O + is distributed further away. The electrolyte ions in EDL distribute in a complex way and as a result of the screening, the interfacial electric field (potential) shows nonuniform behavior.

div.head: ORR mechanism
div.p: We here describe how to derive the free energy of the reaction with ESM-RISM (See the Supporting Information for further details). On Fe-N 4 -C and Co-N 4 -C catalysts (Figure 3a), we assume that ORR follows the four-electron associative mechanism and its elementary steps are written as follows: where * denotes the adsorption site of the catalyst and ∆G i (i = 1, 4) is the corresponding free energy of the reaction. In constructing the free energy diagram for ORR, only three different intermediates were considered ( * OOH, * O, and * OH (Figure 3b-d)), because the steady-state adsorption of the neutral O 2 species does not contribute to the potential dependence. 53,54 In the constant-N e method with ESM-RISM, the free energy of these intermediates is calculated as
div.formula: G X = A tot,X + E ZPE,X + T 0 dT ′ C V,X -T S X(5)
div.p: where A tot,X , E ZPE,X , T 0 dT ′ C V,X and S X are the Helmholtz free energy, the zero-point energy, the integral of the constant-volume heat capacity (C V,X ) over temperature (T ), and the entropy of the intermediate adsorbed species X, respectively. The pH effect is automatically included in A tot within the RISM framework. 36 The free energies of the gas-phase molecules
div.formula: (H 2 , O 2 , and H 2 O) are calculated as G Y = E tot,Y + E ZPE,Y + T 0 dT ′ C p,Y -T S Y , where E tot,Y , T 0 dT ′ C p,Y
div.p: , and S Y are the total energy, the integral is over constant-pressure heat capacity (C p,Y ), and the entropy of the gas-phase species Y . See Table S2 and S3 for the free energy contributions. In the conventional CHE model with a constant number of electrons (constant-N e ), the free energies of the reaction are calculated for the neutral systems, and the effect of the applied potential is introduced as ∆G i (U RHE ) = ∆G i + n|e|U RHE , where ∆G i is the free energy of the reaction for the ith step at pH = 0 and U RHE = 0, with U RHE being the electrode potential with reference to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE), n is the number of electrons transferred in each elementary step, and e is the elementary charge. As described above, the pH effect is automatically included in the free energy in our constant-N e calculation with ESM-RISM, and the reaction free energies are calculated by referencing to the standard hydrogen electrode (SHE), and the potential is converted to
div.formula: U RHE via U RHE = U SHE -k B T ln(10) × pH/|e|.
div.p: In the constant-µ e method with ESM-RISM, the electrode potential can be defined by referencing to the inner potential (see Ref. 36 and the Supporting Information) and it is not necessary to use the equilibrium condition H + (aq)+e -↔ 1/2H 2 (g) at the SHE potential. In the acidic condition, one can assume that H + exists in the form of H 3 O + , and the elementary steps of ORR may be written as
where U on the arrow indicates that the reaction takes place at the constant potential U .
The free energy of the intermediate species X is calculated as
div.formula: G X (U ) = Ω X (U ) + E ZPE,X + T 0 dT ′ C V,X -T S X ,(10)
div.p: where Ω X (U ) = A tot,X -µ e ∆N e is the electronic grand potential at a constant electron chemical potential µ e with reference to U , A tot,X is the Helmholtz free energy, ∆N e is the deviation of the number of electrons from the neutral one, E ZPE,X is the zero-point energy,
div.formula: T 0 dT ′ C V,X is integral of the constant-volume heat capacity C V,X
div.p: , and S X is the entropy. See Table S4 for free energy contributions. See also Refs. 47,[55][56][57] for grand canonical simulations at the electrochemical interfaces, which inspired this work. The free energies of the reaction for each elementary step are expressed as
div.formula: ∆G 1 (U ) = G * OOH (U ) + G H 2 O(l) -G O 2 (g) -G H 3 O + (aq) -µ e -G * (U ) (11) ∆G 2 (U ) = G * O (U ) + 2G H 2 O(l) -G * OOH (U ) -G H 3 O + (aq) -µ e (12
∆G 3 (U ) = G * OH (U ) + G H 2 O(l) -G * O (U ) -G H 3 O + (aq) -µ e (13
∆G 4 (U ) = G * (U ) + 2G H 2 O(l) -G * OH (U ) -G H 3 O + (aq) -µ e (14
div.p: where O 2 (g) and H 2 (g) in the vacuum condition (C p is the constant-pressure heat capacity). We note that the entropies of H 2 O(l) and H 3 O + (aq) species were scaled by a factor of 0.65 as discussed in Refs. 55,59 µ e is the target Fermi energy with reference to applied potential U : At U = 0 V vs RHE in 1 M HCl solution (pH = 0), µ e = -5.33 eV (see the Supporting Information for details). The free energy of the reaction for water formation was calculated to be -4.91 eV using the current formulation (O 2 (g) + 4H 3 O + (aq) + 4e -→ 6H 2 O(l)) and the constant-µ e method, in good agreement with the experimental value of -4.92 eV.
div.formula: G O 2 (g) = E O 2 (g) + E ZPE,O 2 (g) + T 0 dT ′ C P,O 2 (g) -T S O 2 (g)(15)
G * OOH (U ) = Ω * OOH (U ) + E ZPE, * OOH + T 0 dT ′ C V, * OOH -T S * OOH(17)
G * O (U ) = Ω * O (U ) + E ZPE, * O + T 0 dT ′ C V, * O -T S * O (18) G * OH (U ) = Ω * OH (U ) + E ZPE, * OH + T 0 dT ′ C V, * OH -T S * OH(19)
G H 2 O(l) = A H 2 O(l) + E ZPE,H 2 O(l) + T 0 C p,H 2 O(l) dT -T S H 2 O(l)(20)
G H 3 O + (aq) = A H 3 O + (aq) + E ZPE,H 3 O + (aq) + T 0 dT ′ C p,H 3 O + (aq) -T S H 3 O + (aq)(21)
div.p: Computationally, the thermodynamic limiting-potential (U L ) is referred to as the highest potential at which all of the reaction steps in the free energy diagram are downhill, and the difference between the ORR equilibrium potential (U = 1.23 V vs RHE) and U L is called the overpotential, calculated by η = 1.23 -U L . In the constant-N e method, U L can be simply determined as the minimum free energy change among all of the elementary steps of the ORR. In the constant-µ e method, on the other hand, one has to iterate the calculations with different applied potentials U until all of the elementary steps is exothermic. Note that, in the constant-µ e method, the total charge of the system changes depending on the applied potential, in contrast to the conventional CHE/constant-N e method, where the calculations are performed in the neutral condition, and the effect of potential is taken into account in an ad hoc manner.

Figure 4 shows the free energy diagrams of the ORR for TM-N 4 -C obtained using the constant-µ e method as well as the constant-N e method with ESM-RISM (See also Figure S2 ). The potential-determining step (PDS), limiting potential (U L ), and overpotential (η) are summarized in Table 1, along with those obtained in the vacuum condition 45 for comparison. We found that the inclusion of solution is crucial: Indeed, the PDS changes from * O → * OH (∆G 3 ) to * OH → H 2 O (∆G 4 ) for Fe-N 4 -C, and from O 2 → * OOH (∆G 1 ) to * OH → H 2 O (∆G 4 ) for Co-N 4 -C, even without the electrolyte ions (see Figure S3 and Table S5, S6 for detailed comparison). The associated U L (η) changes from 0.66 to 0.51 V (0.57 to 0.72 V) and 0.93 to 0.72 V (0.30 to 0.51 V) for Fe-N 4 -C and Co-N 4 -C in contact with 0.5 M HCl solution, respectively. We found that the effect of the electrolyte concentration on U L and η is not significant within the current ESM-RISM framework, and the limiting potential is almost unchanged when the concentration is increased (see Table S5 and S6 for the results of different electrolyte concentrations).

With the constant-N e method, the predicted η for Co-N 4 -C (0.51 V) is significantly lower than that for Fe-N 4 -C (0.72 V) and competitive to that for Pt(111) (0.48 V), 60 indicating that the former is more reactive than the latter. Our results are in good agreement with the recent results obtained using DFT with implicit solvation models by Haile et al. 61 and Patniboon and Hansen, 5 where η for the Co-N 4 -C is calculated to be 0.57 V and 0.46 V, respectively, and with those by Yang et al., 62 Wang et al., 63 and Patniboon and Hansen, 5 where η for Fe-N 4 -C is predicted to be as high as 0.70 V, 0.83 V, and 0.83 V, respectively.
With the constant-µ e method, we found that there is a competition between * O → * OH and * OH → H 2 O for the Fe-N 4 -C, and O 2 → * OOH and * OH → H 2 O for the Co-N 4 -C.

In the case of Fe-N 4 -C, the PDS changes to * O → * OH (∆G 3 ) with U L (η) is predicted to be 0.78 V (0.45 V), while in the case of Co-N 4 -C, it changes to O 2 → * OOH (∆G 1 ) with U L (η) of 0.80 V (0.43 V). Given its competing nature, we presume that the actual PDS and associated U L may change depending on the local environment (such as defects near the active site or substrate below the carbon surface), which is a subject for future investigation.

Our results indicate that using the constant-µ e method with ESM-RISM, Fe-N 4 -C and Co-N 4 -C show comparable activity, in contrast to those obtained using the constant-N e method. The highest experimental ORR half-wave potential (E 1/2 ) which can be compared with the calculated limiting potential, is reported to be 0.75-0.83 (0.82-0.92) V vs RHE for Fe-N-C 3,64-67 (Co-N-C 66-68 ), and our calculated U L 's are in reasonable agreement, considering the uncertainty of the active sites of the catalysts in the experiments. Note that, the E 1/2 for Co-N-C is reported to be comparable 67 with or slightly lower 66 than that for Fe-N-C, when both the catalysts are synthesized and analyzed within the same experimental procedures. 66,67 Thus, we conclude that the constant-µ e method with ESM-RISM gives results which are more consistent with the experiments and improves upon the conventional simulation based on the constant-N e method. L refers to the limiting potential estimated using the constant-µ e method, while U Ne L refers to that estimated using the constant-N e method.
To understand the origin of the difference between the results obtained using the constant-N e and constant-µ e methods, we first compare the free energies of ORR intermediates (∆G's)

with and without solvent (Table S5 andS6). By the introduction of water without electrolyte ions in the neutral condition with ESM-RISM, the ∆G's for OOH, O, and OH decreases for both TM-N 4 -C's, indicating that water stabilizes all of the adsorbates. By further introduction of the electrolyte ions with ESM-RISM in neutral conditions, the ∆G's decrease consistently (at all of the potentials considered in this work), suggesting that the electrolyte ions contribute to the stabilization of the reaction intermediates. We calculated the charge distribution in the electrolyte solution (Figure S4) and found that the charge of the electrolyte solution distributes according to the charges of the atoms in the molecule and stabilizes the reaction intermediates.

We also calculated ∆G's for the ORR intermediates (Figure 5;Tables S5 andS6) with the constant-µ e and ESM-RISM methods from 0 to 1.23 V vs RHE and found that at 0 V vs RHE, all the adsorbates on TM-N 4 -C are less stable than those in the neutral state obtained using the constant-N e method (larger ∆G's). By increasing the electrode potential, ∆G's decrease as they should. With the constant-N e method (within the CHE framework), ∆G's and thus free energies of the reaction at an applied potential are solely determined by the adsorption free energy under the neutral condition and the potential dependence of -n|e|U regardless of the actual charge state at the potential U . With the constant-µ e method, the potential dependence is almost linear, but the slope deviates from -n|e| depending on the adsorbate, resulting in different limiting potentials/overpotentials from those obtained using the constant-N e method. To understand the difference between the results obtained using the constant-N e and constant-µ e methods, we inspected the total charge and found that the system is positively charged even at U = 0 V vs RHE and gets more positively charged at U > 0 V vs RHE (Figure S5 and S6; Table S7). See also Figure S7 We further analyzed the electronic structures at applied electrode potentials by calcu-

div.head: Summary
div.p: We present a DFT study on the mechanism of ORR on Fe-N 4 -C and Co-N 4 -C in contact with an acidic solution under an applied potential, which has been enabled by ESM-RISM.
We found that the solvent effect is essential in modeling the ORR, as PDS changes from
div.formula: * O → * OH (O 2 → * OOH) to * OH → H 2 O(l) ( * OH → H 2 O(l)) for Fe-N 4 -C (Co-N 4 -C)

div.head: Data availability
div.p: The data underlying this study are openly available in Materials Cloud with the identifier: DOI: 10.24435/materialscloud:7j-bt.

figure.head: Figure 1 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 1: (a) Distribution of H 3 O + and Cl -in HCl solution at 0.7 V vs RHE. (b) Electrostatic potential at 0.7 V vs RHE of Fe-N 4 -C in contact with the HCl solution of the electrolyte concentrations of 1.0, 0.5, and 0.1 M. The Fermi energy (µ e ) is depicted by the horizontal dashed line. The graphene layer is depicted by a ball-and-stick model.

figure.head: Figure 2 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 2: Concentration levels of H + (H 3 O + ) and Cl -ions in 0.5 M HCl at the applied potentials of 0, 0.70, and 1.23 V vs RHE over the Fe-N 4 -C surface adsorbed with OH. Gold, silver, brown, red, and white spheres represent the Fe, N, C, O, and H atoms, respectively. Note that the range of the concentration is changed depending on the electrolyte ion and applied potential for the visibility.

figure.head: Figure 3 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 3: Structures of the active site and ORR intermediates. (a) clean TM-N 4 -C (TM = Fe, Co) active site. (b) * O 2 , (c) * OOH, (d) * O, and (e) * OH. The blue, silver, brown, red, and white atoms represent TM, N, C, O and H, respectively.

is the total energy of O 2 in the gas phase, Ω * (U ) is the electronic grand potential of the clean TM-N 4 -C surfaces ( * ), and Ω * OOH (U ), Ω * O (U ), and Ω * OH (U ) are the electronic grand potentials of the TM-N 4 surfaces with * OOH, * O, and * OH, respectively. A H 2 O(l) and A H 3 O + (aq) are the free energies of solvated H 2 O and H 3 O + determined for each electrolyte solution. E ZPE , T 0 dT ′ C V , and T S for the intermediate species ( * OOH, * O, and * OH) were estimated from the harmonic vibrational frequencies calculated using the atomic simulation environment (ASE 58 ) in the vacuum condition, while E ZPE , T 0 dT ′ C p , and T S for H 2 O(l) and H 3 O + (aq) were estimated using ASE in the implicit solution within ESM-RISM and for

figure.head: Figure 4 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 4: Free energy diagram of the ORR for (a) Fe-N 4 -C and (b) Co-N 4 -C. The results obtained using the constant-µ e and constant-N e methods are plotted with the solid and dashed lines, respectively. U µeL refers to the limiting potential estimated using the constant-µ e method, while U Ne L refers to that estimated using the constant-N e method.

figure.head: Table 1 :
figure.figDesc: The potential-determining step (PDS), limiting potential (U L ), and overpotential (η) of ORR estimated for TM-N 4 -C in vacuum and those for TM-N 4 -C in contact with 0.5 M HCl solution obtained using the constant-N e and constant-µ e methods with ESM-RISM. a The results obtained with the constant-N e and vacuum condition are taken from Ref. 45 constant-N e (vacuum) a constant-N e (ESM-RISM) constant-µ e (ESM-RISM) Fe-N 4 -C PDS

and S8 for the change in the solvent charge distribution in response to the applied potential. As a result, TM-N 4 -C adsorbed with the ORR intermediate becomes less and less stable relative to clean TM-N 4 -C as U is increased (Figure S9), and the slope of the potential dependence of ∆G's deviates from -n|e|.

figure.head: Figure 5 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 5: Free energies of the reaction intermediates relative to the final state (∆G's) as functions of applied potentials (U ) for (a) Fe-N 4 -C and (b) Co-N 4 -C at 0.5 M HCl obtained using the constant-N e (∆G Ne ) and constant-µ e (∆G µe ) methods. The lines are guides for eyes.

when the implicit solvent is introduced, but the effect of the electrolyte concentration is minor on the limiting potential. We also found that, with the constant-µ e method combined with ESM-RISM, the reaction intermediates become competitive, and the resulting PDSs are * O → * OH and O 2 (g) → * OOH for Fe-N 4 -C and Co-N 4 -C, respectively. The calculated limiting potentials are 0.78 and 0.80 V for both Fe-N 4 -C and Co-N 4 -C, which are comparable, in contrast to the results obtained with the constant-N e method and those in the literature, in which superior catalytic activity of Co-N 4 -C has been predicted. The origin of the discrepancy between the results obtained with the two methods is mainly the charge state (different from the neutral one) at a fixed electrode potential, which can be determined through the constant-µ e method with ESM-RISM. This work clarifies the roles of solvent and electrolyte ions within the RISM framework and demonstrates the importance of the variable charge state through the constant potential calculation. However, studying specific adsorption of electrolyte ion and the effect on the active site51,52,69 is challenging with the plain RISM framework. Further investigation of the electrolyte effect on the catalytic activity by e.g., explicitly treating anions with ESM-RISM will be of great interest. Nevertheless, we anticipate that this work is a step toward more realistic simulations of the electrochemical reactions.AcknowledgementThis work was partly supported by Grant in Aid for Scientific Research on Innovative Areas "Hydrogenomics" (Grant No. JP18H05519) and "Program for Promoting Researches on the Supercomputer Fugaku" (Fugaku battery & Fuel Cell Project) from the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science, and Technology, Japan (MEXT). A.F.Z.A. acknowledges the financial support from MEXT. Calculations were performed using the facilities of the Supercomputer Center, the Institute for Solid State Physics, the University of Tokyo, and of the Cybermedia Center, Osaka University.

div.head: Supporting Information Available
div.p: Supporting Information is available. Computational methods including the computational detail, SHE potential, formulation of the free energy of the reaction with constant-N e and constant-µ e methods; Comparison of free energy diagrams obtained using different methods;

analytic.title: Noble-metal-free Fe-N/C Catalyst for Highly Efficient Oxygen Reduction Reaction under Both Alkaline and Acidic Conditions
Iron-Based Catalysts with Improved Oxygen Reduction Activity in Polymer Electrolyte Fuel Cells
Performance Enhancement and Degradation Mechanism Identification of a Single-Atom Co-N-C Catalyst for Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cells

Identification of the Highly Active Co-N 4 Coordination Motif for Selective Oxygen Reduction to Hydrogen Peroxide
Identification of Catalytic Sites for Oxygen Reduction in Iron-and Nitrogen-Doped Graphene Materials
Identification of Catalytic Sites in Cobalt-Nitrogen-Carbon Materials for the Oxygen Reduction Reaction
High Loading of Single Atomic Iron Sites in Fe-NC Oxygen Reduction Catalysts for Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cells
Nitrogen-Doped Carbon Xerogels Catalyst for Oxygen Reduction Reaction: Improved Structural and Catalytic Activity by Enhancing Nitrogen Species and Cobalt Insertion
Iron Atom-Cluster Interactions Increase Activity and Improve Durability in Fe-N-C Fuel Cells
Identifying the Impact of the Covalent-Bonded Carbon Matrix to FeN 4 Sites for Acidic Oxygen Reduction
Cobalt-Doped Carbon Xerogel with Different Initial pH Values Toward Oxygen Reduction
Effect of Carbon Precursor and Initial pH on Cobalt-Doped Carbon Xerogel for Oxygen Reduction
Electrocatalysis of Oxygen Reduction and Small Alcohol Oxidation in Alkaline Media
Influence of Inner-and Outer-Sphere Electron Transfer Mechanisms During Electrocatalysis of Oxygen Reduction in Alkaline Media
Elucidating Oxygen Reduction Active Sites in Pyrolyzed Metal-Nitrogen Coordinated Non-Precious-Metal Electrocatalyst Systems
Activity Descriptor Identification for Oxygen Reduction on Nonprecious Electrocatalysts: Linking Surface Science to Coordination Chemistry
pH Effect on the H 2 O 2 -Induced Deactivation of Fe-N-C Catalysts
Improving the Activity of M-N 4 Catalysts for the Oxygen Reduction Reaction by Electrolyte Adsorption
Probing the Effects of Acid Electrolyte Anions on Electrocatalyst Activity and Selectivity for the Oxygen Reduction Reaction
Critical Role of Explicit Inclusion of Solvent and Electrode Potential in the Electrochemical Description of Nitrogen Reduction
Origin of the Overpotential for Oxygen Reduction at a Fuel-Cell Cathode
Ab initio Simulation of Charged Slabs at Constant Chemical Potential
First-Principles Calculations of Charged Surfaces and Interfaces: A Plane-Wave Nonrepeated Slab Approach
First-Principles Molecular Dynamics at a Constant Electrode Potential
Grand Canonical Electronic Density-Functional Theory: Algorithms and Applications to Electrochemistry
First Principles Reaction Modeling of the Electrochemical Interface: Consideration and Calculation of a Tunable Surface Potential from Atomic and Electronic Structure
Electrode Dynamics from First Principles
Electronic Structure Calculations of Liquid-Solid Interfaces: Combination of Density Functional Theory and Modified Poisson-Boltzmann Theory
